_meta.purpose: Themed nickname pools for Claude Code subagents — a userspace port of Codex's per-instance `nickname_candidates`.
_meta.invariant: Every name is globally unique across all categories (enforced by Registry.validate()).

bios.Magellan: Portuguese explorer whose expedition completed the first circumnavigation of the Earth, 1519-22.
bios.Shackleton: Irish-British explorer famed for his failed Antarctic crossing and the Endurance survival voyage.
bios.Amundsen: Norwegian explorer who led the first expedition to reach the South Pole, in 1911.
bios.Cook: British Royal Navy captain who charted the Pacific across three major voyages of exploration.
bios.Polo: Venetian merchant traveler whose account of his journey to Kublai Khan's China became legendary.
bios.Columbus: Genoese navigator whose 1492 Atlantic voyage opened sustained European contact with the Americas.
bios.Livingstone: Scottish missionary explorer who mapped much of central Africa, including Victoria Falls.
bios.Hillary: New Zealand mountaineer who, with Tenzing Norgay, made the first confirmed ascent of Everest, 1953.
bios.Earhart: American aviator, the first woman to fly solo across the Atlantic Ocean.
bios.IbnBattuta: Moroccan scholar who traveled roughly 75,000 miles across Africa, Asia, and the Middle East.
bios.ZhengHe: Ming dynasty Chinese admiral who led seven massive maritime treasure voyages across Asia and Africa.
bios.Lewis: American army officer who co-led the Lewis and Clark expedition across the western United States.
bios.Clark: American officer who co-led, with Meriwether Lewis, the Corps of Discovery expedition west.
bios.Drake: English sea captain, the first Englishman to circumnavigate the globe, 1577-80.
bios.Hudson: English navigator who explored the bay and river bearing his name while seeking a Northwest Passage.
bios.Cabot: Italian navigator whose 1497 voyage for England reached the coast of North America.
bios.DaGama: Portuguese explorer who opened the first direct sea route from Europe to India.
bios.Nansen: Norwegian explorer and scientist known for Arctic expeditions and later Nobel-winning refugee work.
bios.Scott: British Royal Navy officer who led a fatal race to the South Pole, arriving just after Amundsen.
bios.Boone: American frontiersman who blazed the Wilderness Road and helped settle Kentucky.
bios.Sacagawea: Shoshone woman who guided and interpreted for the Lewis and Clark expedition.
bios.Tenzing: Nepali Sherpa mountaineer who, with Edmund Hillary, first summited Mount Everest in 1953.
bios.Cousteau: French naval officer and oceanographer who co-invented the Aqua-Lung and pioneered undersea filming.
bios.Gagarin: Soviet cosmonaut, the first human to journey into outer space, in 1961.
bios.Heyerdahl: Norwegian adventurer who sailed the Kon-Tiki raft across the Pacific to test migration theories.
bios.Bering: Danish-born Russian navigator who explored the strait between Siberia and Alaska now named for him.
bios.Tasman: Dutch explorer who charted Tasmania, New Zealand, and other Pacific coasts for the Dutch East India Co.
bios.Xuanzang: Chinese Buddhist monk whose overland pilgrimage to India shaped Buddhist scripture and travel lore.
bios.Pytheas: Greek geographer from Massalia who sailed to Britain, one of antiquity's boldest recorded voyages.
bios.Frobisher: English privateer and explorer who made three voyages seeking a Northwest Passage through Canada.

bios.Turing: British mathematician who formalized computation and helped break the Enigma cipher in WWII.
bios.Dijkstra: Dutch computer scientist known for shortest-path algorithms and structured programming principles.
bios.Knuth: American computer scientist, author of The Art of Computer Programming and creator of TeX.
bios.Hopper: American computer scientist who pioneered compilers and helped develop the COBOL language.
bios.Ada: Ada Lovelace, English mathematician who wrote the first published algorithm, for Babbage's engine.
bios.Torvalds: Finnish-American software engineer who created the Linux kernel and the Git version control system.
bios.Ritchie: American computer scientist who created the C programming language and co-created Unix.
bios.Thompson: American computer scientist who co-created Unix and later designed the Go programming language.
bios.Kernighan: Canadian computer scientist who co-authored the C programming language book and named Unix.
bios.BernersLee: British computer scientist who invented the World Wide Web, HTML, and HTTP.
bios.Stallman: American programmer who founded the GNU Project and the free software movement.
bios.Carmack: American programmer known for pioneering 3D game engines at id Software, including Doom and Quake.
bios.Guido: Guido van Rossum, Dutch programmer who created the Python programming language.
bios.Stroustrup: Danish computer scientist who designed and implemented the C plus plus programming language.
bios.Backus: American computer scientist who developed FORTRAN and co-created Backus-Naur Form notation.
bios.McCarthy: American computer scientist who coined the term artificial intelligence and created Lisp.
bios.AlanKay: American computer scientist who pioneered object-oriented programming and the Smalltalk language.
bios.Engelbart: American engineer who invented the computer mouse and demoed hypertext in the 1968 Mother of All Demos.
bios.Wozniak: American electronics engineer who co-founded Apple and designed its early personal computers.
bios.Liskov: American computer scientist known for the Liskov substitution principle in object-oriented design.
bios.Lamport: American computer scientist known for foundational work on distributed systems and creating LaTeX.
bios.Cerf: American computer scientist who co-designed the TCP/IP protocols underlying the internet.
bios.AlKhwarizmi: Persian polymath whose name gave us the word algorithm; wrote foundational works on algebra.
bios.Babbage: English mathematician who designed the Analytical Engine, a mechanical precursor to the computer.
bios.Matz: Yukihiro Matsumoto, Japanese computer scientist who created the Ruby programming language.
bios.Gosling: Canadian computer scientist known as the father of the Java programming language.
bios.Hejlsberg: Danish software engineer who designed Turbo Pascal, Delphi, C sharp, and TypeScript.
bios.Aho: Canadian computer scientist who co-authored the Dragon Book and co-created the AWK language.
bios.Ullman: American computer scientist known for foundational work on compilers and automata theory.
bios.Hamilton: Margaret Hamilton, American computer scientist who led the software team for NASA's Apollo missions.
bios.Perlis: American computer scientist, first recipient of the Turing Award, known for programming language theory.
bios.Iverson: Canadian computer scientist who created the APL programming language.
bios.Hoare: British computer scientist who developed the Quicksort algorithm and Hoare logic.

bios.Darwin: English naturalist who proposed evolution by natural selection in On the Origin of Species.
bios.Newton: English physicist and mathematician who formulated the laws of motion and universal gravitation.
bios.Einstein: German-born physicist who developed the theories of special and general relativity.
bios.Curie: Marie Curie, Polish-French physicist and chemist who pioneered research on radioactivity.
bios.Feynman: American theoretical physicist known for quantum electrodynamics and vivid physics teaching.
bios.Faraday: English scientist whose experiments established electromagnetic induction and field theory.
bios.Bohr: Danish physicist who developed the quantum model of the atom.
bios.Heisenberg: German physicist known for the uncertainty principle in quantum mechanics.
bios.Rosalind: Rosalind Franklin, British chemist whose X-ray images were key to discovering DNA's structure.
bios.Mendel: Austrian friar and botanist whose pea-plant experiments founded the science of genetics.
bios.Pasteur: French chemist and microbiologist who developed pasteurization and the germ theory of disease.
bios.Galileo: Italian astronomer and physicist who championed heliocentrism and improved the telescope.
bios.Kepler: German astronomer who formulated the laws of planetary motion.
bios.Hubble: American astronomer who showed the universe is expanding and that other galaxies exist beyond ours.
bios.Hawking: British theoretical physicist known for work on black holes and cosmology despite ALS.
bios.Maxwell: Scottish physicist who unified electricity, magnetism, and light into classical electromagnetism.
bios.Planck: German physicist whose quantum hypothesis founded quantum theory.
bios.Rutherford: New Zealand physicist who discovered the atomic nucleus and pioneered nuclear physics.
bios.Fermi: Italian physicist who built the first nuclear reactor and worked on quantum theory.
bios.Dirac: British theoretical physicist who predicted antimatter and helped found quantum mechanics.
bios.Schrodinger: Austrian physicist who formulated wave mechanics and the famous cat thought experiment.
bios.Volta: Italian physicist who invented the electric battery.
bios.Kelvin: Scottish physicist who established the absolute temperature scale bearing his name.
bios.Herschel: German-British astronomer who discovered the planet Uranus and infrared radiation.
bios.Meitner: Austrian-Swedish physicist who co-discovered nuclear fission but was denied the Nobel for it.
bios.Goodall: British primatologist whose long-term chimpanzee studies transformed primate research.
bios.Sagan: American astronomer and science communicator who popularized cosmology through Cosmos.
bios.Chandrasekhar: Indian-American astrophysicist known for the limit on white dwarf star mass bearing his name.
bios.McClintock: American scientist who discovered transposons, mobile genetic elements, in maize.
bios.Pauling: American chemist who advanced quantum chemistry and won Nobel Prizes in chemistry and peace.
bios.Avicenna: Persian polymath physician whose Canon of Medicine shaped medical science for centuries.
bios.Copernicus: Polish astronomer who proposed the heliocentric model placing the Sun at the center.

reflect.blurb: Wonders about a project's inner workings, design rationale, meaning, and tradeoffs — not coding, not research.

bios.Socrates: Ancient Greek philosopher known for the Socratic method of questioning, known through Plato's dialogues.
bios.Plato: Ancient Greek philosopher who founded the Academy and wrote foundational dialogues on justice and forms.
bios.Aristotle: Ancient Greek philosopher whose works shaped logic, ethics, and natural science for centuries.
bios.Kant: German philosopher who argued reason structures experience, in works like the Critique of Pure Reason.
bios.Hegel: German philosopher known for dialectical reasoning and a systematic account of historical progress.
bios.Nietzsche: German philosopher known for critiques of morality and the concept of the will to power.
bios.Descartes: French philosopher known for methodical doubt and the line 'I think, therefore I am'.
bios.Hume: Scottish philosopher who advanced empiricism and skepticism about causation.
bios.Spinoza: Dutch philosopher who argued for a single substance identified with both God and nature.
bios.Leibniz: German philosopher and mathematician who co-invented calculus and argued for a best possible world.
bios.Wittgenstein: Austrian-British philosopher known for work on language, logic, and meaning as use.
bios.Heidegger: German philosopher known for Being and Time and inquiry into the nature of existence.
bios.Sartre: French philosopher and writer who championed existentialism and radical human freedom.
bios.Confucius: Chinese philosopher whose teachings on ethics and social harmony shaped East Asian thought.
bios.Laozi: Legendary Chinese sage credited as founder of Taoism and author of the Tao Te Ching.
bios.Aquinas: Italian friar and theologian who synthesized Aristotelian philosophy with Christian doctrine.
bios.Locke: English philosopher whose theories of natural rights and empiricism shaped liberal political thought.
bios.Rousseau: Genevan philosopher whose Social Contract shaped modern political and educational thought.
bios.Camus: French-Algerian philosopher and writer known for absurdism, as in The Myth of Sisyphus.
bios.Arendt: German-American philosopher known for The Origins of Totalitarianism and the 'banality of evil'.
bios.Beauvoir: French philosopher and writer whose The Second Sex became foundational to feminist theory.
bios.Aurelius: Roman emperor and Stoic philosopher whose private journal Meditations survives as a classic text.
bios.Epictetus: Greek Stoic philosopher, born a slave, whose teachings on control and acceptance shaped Stoicism.
bios.Seneca: Roman Stoic philosopher and statesman known for essays and letters on ethics and mortality.
bios.Kierkegaard: Danish philosopher often called the father of existentialism, known for Fear and Trembling.
bios.Schopenhauer: German philosopher known for pessimism and the concept of will as the driving force of reality.
bios.Hypatia: Alexandrian philosopher and mathematician, a leading Neoplatonist thinker of late antiquity.
bios.Averroes: Andalusian polymath whose commentaries on Aristotle influenced medieval European philosophy.
bios.Zhuangzi: Chinese philosopher whose writings, blending humor and paradox, shaped Taoist thought.
bios.Montaigne: French Renaissance philosopher who popularized the essay as a form of self-examination.
bios.Weil: Simone Weil, French philosopher and mystic known for writings on affliction, attention, and justice.

bios.Holmes: Arthur Conan Doyle's brilliant Baker Street consulting detective, famed for cold, precise deduction.
bios.Poirot: Agatha Christie's fastidious Belgian detective who solves murders using his little grey cells.
bios.Marple: Agatha Christie's sharp elderly amateur sleuth from a quiet English village, underestimated by all.
bios.Dupin: Edgar Allan Poe's original armchair detective, solving Parisian crimes through pure ratiocination.
bios.Columbo: Peter Falk's rumpled TV homicide lieutenant who disarms suspects with just one more question.
bios.Spade: Dashiell Hammett's hard-boiled San Francisco private eye at the center of The Maltese Falcon.
bios.Marlowe: Raymond Chandler's cynical, wisecracking private detective prowling 1940s Los Angeles noir.
bios.Nancy: Teenage amateur sleuth of the classic American mystery series, ever curious, brave, and clever.
bios.Magnum: Tom Selleck's mustached Hawaii-based private investigator from a beloved 1980s American TV series.
bios.Clouseau: Peter Sellers' bumbling but improbably lucky French police inspector from The Pink Panther films.
bios.Wimsey: Dorothy L. Sayers' witty aristocratic English lord who solves murders as an amateur detective.
bios.Maigret: Georges Simenon's pipe-smoking Parisian police detective, patient, observant, and quietly relentless.
bios.Wolfe: Rex Stout's orchid-growing, armchair-bound genius detective who almost never leaves his brownstone.
bios.Vance: S.S. Van Dine's erudite, aesthete amateur detective solving crimes among 1920s New York high society.
bios.Fandorin: Boris Akunin's brilliant 19th century Russian detective, hero of a celebrated historical mystery series.
bios.Morse: Colin Dexter's opera-loving, crossword-solving Oxford police detective, brilliant but irritable.
bios.Rebus: Ian Rankin's hard-drinking, rule-bending Edinburgh police detective, moody, dogged, and sharp.
bios.Bosch: Michael Connelly's relentless LAPD detective who lives by everybody counts or nobody counts.
bios.Scarpetta: Patricia Cornwell's meticulous forensic pathologist who solves murders through the science of autopsy.
bios.Archer: Ross Macdonald's introspective, morally serious hard-boiled California private investigator.
bios.Hammer: Mickey Spillane's brutal, vengeance-driven pulp-fiction private eye of postwar New York City.
bios.Millhone: Sue Grafton's tenacious California private investigator from the beloved alphabet mystery series.
bios.Goren: Vincent D'Onofrio's unorthodox, hyper-observant NYPD detective from Law & Order: Criminal Intent.
bios.Brenner: Wolf Haas's laconic Austrian ex-cop turned private detective in a dryly comic novel series.

test.blurb: QA, edge cases, fuzzing, adversarial probing, evals.

bios.Loki: Norse trickster god, shapeshifter and mischief-maker who schemes among the Aesir.
bios.Anansi: West African spider trickster god of Akan folklore, wins wisdom and stories through pure cunning.
bios.Coyote: Trickster figure of many Native American traditions, clever and foolish by turns, creator and fool.
bios.Hermes: Greek messenger god, patron of thieves, travelers, and quick, clever deception.
bios.Prometheus: Greek Titan who outwitted the gods and stole fire to gift it to struggling humanity.
bios.Sisyphus: Greek king punished with an eternal boulder to push, famed for repeatedly outwitting death.
bios.Eris: Greek goddess of strife and discord, sparked the Trojan War with a golden apple of chaos.
bios.Puck: Mischievous fairy sprite of English folklore, star trickster of A Midsummer Night's Dream.
bios.Reynard: Cunning fox trickster hero of medieval European fable cycles, outwitting kings and beasts alike.
bios.Kokopelli: Hump-backed, flute-playing fertility trickster deity of Southwestern Native American traditions.
bios.Maui: Polynesian demigod trickster who snared the sun and fished up islands from the sea.
bios.Susanoo: Japanese storm god and trickster, the unruly brother of the sun goddess Amaterasu.
bios.Wukong: Mischievous Monkey King of Chinese myth, trickster hero of the epic Journey to the West.
bios.Eulenspiegel: Prankster folk hero of German legend, famed for elaborate practical jokes on the pompous.
bios.Brer: Cunning trickster rabbit of African American folktales, always one step ahead of his foes.
bios.Legba: West African and Vodou trickster spirit who guards the crossroads between worlds.
bios.Nasreddin: Witty, foolish-wise trickster teacher of Middle Eastern folklore, hiding lessons in jokes.
bios.Iktomi: Lakota spider trickster spirit who teaches hard lessons through cunning and mischief.
bios.Kitsune: Shapeshifting fox spirit of Japanese folklore, famed for cunning illusions and clever pranks.
bios.Tanuki: Shapeshifting raccoon dog spirit of Japanese folklore, a jovial, mischievous prankster.
bios.Pan: Greek god of the wild and shepherds, mischievous piper linked to sudden fear in flocks.
bios.Set: Egyptian god of chaos and storms, trickster rival of his brother Osiris.
bios.Veles: Slavic god of the underworld and cattle, a cunning trickster rival of the thunder god Perun.
bios.Baubo: Greek folkloric old woman who made the grieving goddess Demeter laugh with a bawdy jest.

review.blurb: Code review, critique, correctness scrutiny, verdicts.

bios.Solomon: Biblical king of Israel renowned for wise judgment, famed for the baby-splitting ruling.
bios.Solon: Ancient Athenian statesman and lawgiver whose reforms underpinned early Athenian democracy.
bios.Hammurabi: Babylonian king who issued one of history's earliest written law codes, carved in stone.
bios.Justinian: Byzantine emperor who codified Roman law into the enduring Corpus Juris Civilis.
bios.Marshall: US Chief Justice who established judicial review through the landmark Marbury v. Madison ruling.
bios.Cardozo: Influential US Supreme Court justice celebrated for his eloquent, philosophical legal opinions.
bios.Brandeis: US Supreme Court justice and pioneering advocate of the constitutional right to privacy.
bios.Ginsburg: US Supreme Court justice and pioneering champion of gender equality under the law.
bios.Thurgood: First African American justice to serve on the US Supreme Court, formerly a civil rights lawyer.
bios.OConnor: First woman ever to serve as a justice on the US Supreme Court.
bios.Scalia: US Supreme Court justice known for sharp wit and staunchly originalist legal reasoning.
bios.Story: Influential early US Supreme Court justice and legal scholar who shaped American jurisprudence.
bios.Blackstone: English jurist whose Commentaries on the Laws of England shaped modern common law.
bios.Bracton: Medieval English judge credited with an early, influential treatise on the common law.
bios.Mansfield: English judge whose rulings shaped modern commercial and contract law doctrine.
bios.Denning: Influential, reform-minded English appellate judge known for bold, plainspoken rulings.
bios.Warren: US Chief Justice who led the court through Brown v. Board of Education and other landmark rulings.
bios.Portia: Shakespeare's clever lawyer-in-disguise from The Merchant of Venice, famed for her mercy speech.
bios.Bao: Revered Song dynasty Chinese judge famed across folklore for incorruptible justice.
bios.Draco: Ancient Athenian lawgiver known for an infamously harsh penal code and severe punishments.
bios.Lycurgus: Legendary Spartan lawgiver credited with shaping Sparta's austere, disciplined constitution.
bios.Ulpian: Roman jurist whose extensive legal writings became a pillar of the later Corpus Juris Civilis.
bios.Papinian: Roman jurist regarded by many scholars as antiquity's greatest legal mind.
bios.Sotomayor: First Latina justice to serve on the US Supreme Court, formerly a federal appellate judge.

security.blurb: Security audit, threat modeling, hardening, guardrails.

bios.Argus: Hundred-eyed giant watchman of Greek myth, famed for never fully closing every eye.
bios.Cerberus: Three-headed hound of Greek myth who guards the gates of the underworld.
bios.Heimdall: Norse watchman god who guards the rainbow bridge to Asgard with unmatched senses.
bios.Horus: Egyptian falcon-headed sky god, protector of kingship and rightful order.
bios.Bastet: Egyptian cat goddess, protector of the home and guardian against evil spirits.
bios.Aegis: Protective shield of Zeus and Athena in Greek myth, a lasting symbol of divine defense.
bios.Fafnir: Dwarf-turned-dragon of Norse legend who guards a vast, cursed hoard of treasure.
bios.Ladon: Many-headed serpent dragon of Greek myth who guarded the golden apples of the Hesperides.
bios.Talos: Giant bronze automaton of Greek myth who tirelessly patrolled the shores of Crete.
bios.Anubis: Egyptian jackal-headed god who guards the dead and weighs the heart of every soul.
bios.Tyr: Norse god of law and war who sacrificed his hand to bind the monstrous wolf Fenrir.
bios.Athena: Greek goddess of wisdom and strategic warfare, patron guardian of Athens.
bios.Garuda: Divine eagle-man of Hindu myth, mount of Vishnu and fierce natural enemy of serpents.
bios.Hachiman: Japanese Shinto god of war, divine protector and guardian of warriors.
bios.Sekhmet: Egyptian lioness goddess of war and fierce protection, also revered as a healer.
bios.Fenrir: Monstrous wolf of Norse myth, chained by the gods until the coming of Ragnarok.
bios.Vidar: Silent, mighty Norse god fated to avenge his father Odin at the final battle of Ragnarok.
bios.Perseus: Greek hero who slew the gorgon Medusa and rescued the chained princess Andromeda.
bios.Durga: Hindu warrior goddess created by the gods to defeat a demon that no man could kill.
bios.Nemesis: Greek goddess of retribution, delivers balance and downfall to the arrogant and proud.
bios.Golem: Clay guardian creature of Jewish folklore, animated by mystics to protect its community.
bios.Shu: Egyptian god of air who holds up the sky, separating it from the earth below.

design.blurb: Frontend, UI/UX, visual and interaction design.

bios.DaVinci: Renaissance painter, inventor, and polymath, creator of the Mona Lisa and countless designs.
bios.Michelangelo: Renaissance sculptor and painter who created the Sistine Chapel ceiling and the statue David.
bios.Rembrandt: Dutch Golden Age painter and master of light and shadow, known for deep psychological portraits.
bios.Monet: French Impressionist painter famed for water lilies and ever-shifting studies of light.
bios.Picasso: Spanish artist and co-founder of Cubism, one of modern art's most restless innovators.
bios.Kahlo: Mexican painter known for vivid, deeply personal self-portraits exploring identity and pain.
bios.Hokusai: Japanese ukiyo-e master behind the iconic woodblock print The Great Wave off Kanagawa.
bios.Klimt: Austrian Symbolist painter famed for gilded, ornate works such as The Kiss.
bios.Matisse: French painter and leader of the bold, vividly colored Fauvist art movement.
bios.OKeeffe: American modernist painter celebrated for magnified flowers and stark desert landscapes.
bios.Vermeer: Dutch master painter of quiet, light-filled domestic scenes, including Girl with a Pearl Earring.
bios.Caravaggio: Italian Baroque painter famed for dramatic light, deep shadow, and raw realism.
bios.Rothko: American painter known for luminous, meditative color-field canvases meant to overwhelm the viewer.
bios.Kandinsky: Pioneering abstract artist who linked color and form to sound, music, and emotion.
bios.Rams: German industrial designer famed for his ten enduring principles of good design.
bios.PaulRand: American graphic designer behind the iconic IBM, ABC, and UPS corporate logos.
bios.Eames: American design duo celebrated for iconic mid-century modern furniture and architecture.
bios.Vignelli: Italian designer behind the enduring New York City subway map and signage system.
bios.SaulBass: American graphic designer famed for pioneering film title sequences and corporate logos.
bios.Mucha: Czech Art Nouveau artist famed for ornate, flowing decorative posters and illustrations.
bios.Escher: Dutch artist known for impossible shapes, tessellations, and mind-bending optical illusions.
bios.Gaudi: Catalan architect behind the organic, surreal, still-unfinished Sagrada Familia basilica.
bios.Basquiat: American Neo-Expressionist painter of raw, symbolic works rooted in street art.
bios.Warhol: American Pop artist famed for Campbell's soup cans and silkscreen celebrity portraits.

data.blurb: Data engineering, ML, analysis, statistics, visualization.

bios.Gauss: German mathematician dubbed prince of mathematicians; foundational work in number theory and statistics.
bios.Euler: Swiss mathematician who introduced modern notation (e, i, f(x)) and founded graph theory.
bios.Riemann: German mathematician whose geometry underlies general relativity; his hypothesis on primes remains unproven.
bios.Fermat: French lawyer and mathematician whose margin note on a theorem took 358 years to prove.
bios.Fibonacci: Medieval Italian mathematician who introduced Hindu-Arabic numerals to Europe and a famous number sequence.
bios.Bayes: English minister behind the theorem for updating probabilities as new evidence arrives.
bios.Fisher: British statistician who pioneered ANOVA and modern experimental design.
bios.Tukey: American statistician who coined the words bit and software, and pioneered exploratory data analysis.
bios.Pascal: French mathematician of the famous triangle, and co-founder of probability theory.
bios.Laplace: French scholar who applied probability to celestial mechanics and gave us a namesake transform.
bios.Kolmogorov: Soviet mathematician who laid the axiomatic foundations of modern probability theory.
bios.Erdos: Hungarian mathematician of legendary output, wandering the world with no fixed home, paper in hand.
bios.Hilbert: German mathematician who posed 23 landmark problems that shaped 20th-century mathematics.
bios.Cantor: German mathematician who founded set theory and proved some infinities are bigger than others.
bios.Noether: German mathematician whose theorem links symmetry to conservation laws in physics.
bios.Ramanujan: Self-taught Indian mathematician whose notebooks of formulas stunned Cambridge scholars.
bios.Nightingale: English nurse and statistician who invented the polar area chart to visualize mortality data.
bios.Gosset: British statistician who published as Student while working at a Dublin brewery; father of the t-distribution.
bios.Galois: French mathematician who founded group theory and died in a duel at just twenty.
bios.Poincare: French polymath, a founder of chaos theory and topology, and a rival in the race to relativity.
bios.Bernoulli: Swiss family of mathematicians behind fluid dynamics and the law of large numbers.
bios.Lagrange: Italian-French mathematician who reformulated mechanics and advanced calculus of variations.
bios.Markov: Russian mathematician whose chains model systems where the next step depends only on the current one.
bios.Nash: American mathematician whose equilibrium concept transformed game theory and economics.
bios.Aryabhata: Ancient Indian astronomer-mathematician who computed pi to four decimals and pioneered trigonometry.
bios.Brahmagupta: Indian mathematician who first gave formal rules for arithmetic with zero and negative numbers.
bios.Shannon: American mathematician-engineer who founded information theory, the math behind digital communication.
bios.Boole: English mathematician whose algebra of true and false underlies all modern computer logic.
bios.Fourier: French mathematician who showed any signal can be built from sums of simple waves.
bios.Cauchy: French mathematician who brought rigor to calculus and founded complex analysis.

orchestrate.blurb: Planning, coordination, task breakdown, multi-agent direction.

bios.SunTzu: Ancient Chinese strategist and author of The Art of War, still studied in military academies today.
bios.Napoleon: French emperor and general whose campaigns reshaped Europe before ending at Waterloo.
bios.Hannibal: Carthaginian general who marched an army with war elephants across the Alps to strike Rome.
bios.Caesar: Roman general and statesman who conquered Gaul and reshaped the Republic into an empire.
bios.Alexander: Macedonian king who built an empire stretching from Greece to India before age thirty.
bios.Zhukov: Soviet marshal whose armies broke the Wehrmacht at Stalingrad and took Berlin.
bios.Patton: American general famed for fast-moving tank offensives across WWII Europe.
bios.Rommel: German field marshal nicknamed the Desert Fox for his campaigns in North Africa.
bios.Scipio: Roman general who defeated Hannibal at the Battle of Zama, ending the Second Punic War.
bios.Nelson: British admiral who shattered French and Spanish fleets at Trafalgar, dying in the moment of victory.
bios.Wellington: British general and statesman who defeated Napoleon at Waterloo.
bios.Khalid: Early Islamic general known as the Sword of God, undefeated across dozens of battles.
bios.Saladin: Muslim sultan who united Egypt and Syria and recaptured Jerusalem from the Crusaders.
bios.Genghis: Mongol ruler who unified the steppe tribes and founded history's largest contiguous empire.
bios.Belisarius: Byzantine general under Justinian who reconquered North Africa and Italy for the empire.
bios.Themistocles: Athenian statesman and general whose naval strategy won the decisive Battle of Salamis.
bios.Leonidas: Spartan king who led the famed last stand of the 300 at Thermopylae.
bios.Pericles: Athenian statesman-general who led the city through its golden age of art and democracy.
bios.Clausewitz: Prussian general and theorist whose treatise On War remains a cornerstone of strategic thought.
bios.Marlborough: English general whose victory at Blenheim broke French dominance in Europe.
bios.Timur: Central Asian conqueror who built a vast empire through relentless campaigns.
bios.Joan: The Maid of Orleans: a French peasant girl who led armies against English invaders and was later canonized.
bios.Boudica: British Celtic queen who led a fierce revolt of the tribes against Roman occupation.
bios.Kutuzov: Russian field marshal whose scorched-earth strategy broke Napoleon's invasion of Russia.
bios.Nimitz: American admiral who commanded the Pacific Fleet to victory across WWII.
bios.Cyrus: Persian king who founded the Achaemenid Empire and was known for tolerant rule over conquered peoples.
bios.Subutai: Mongol general under Genghis Khan whose campaigns stretched from China to Europe.

bios.Shakespeare: English playwright and poet behind Hamlet and Romeo and Juliet, the most quoted writer in English.
bios.Tolstoy: Russian novelist whose sprawling War and Peace remains a landmark of world literature.
bios.Austen: English novelist of sharp wit, best known for Pride and Prejudice.
bios.Orwell: English novelist whose 1984 and Animal Farm defined dystopian political fiction.
bios.Hemingway: American novelist famed for spare prose and The Old Man and the Sea.
bios.Borges: Argentine writer of labyrinthine short fiction that helped invent magical realism.
bios.Woolf: English modernist novelist known for stream-of-consciousness prose in Mrs Dalloway.
bios.Twain: American novelist and humorist behind The Adventures of Huckleberry Finn.
bios.Kafka: Czech novelist whose surreal, anxious tales gave rise to a term for nightmarish bureaucracy.
bios.Dickens: English novelist of Victorian social realism, author of Oliver Twist and Great Expectations.
bios.Homer: Ancient Greek poet credited with the epic Iliad and Odyssey.
bios.Dante: Italian poet whose Divine Comedy imagined a journey through hell, purgatory, and heaven.
bios.Cervantes: Spanish novelist whose Don Quixote is often called the first modern novel.
bios.Chekhov: Russian playwright and short-story master known for The Cherry Orchard.
bios.Dostoevsky: Russian novelist of moral and psychological depth, author of Crime and Punishment.
bios.Proust: French novelist whose multi-volume In Search of Lost Time explores memory at length.
bios.Joyce: Irish novelist whose stream-of-consciousness Ulysses reshaped the modern novel.
bios.Melville: American novelist behind the whaling epic Moby-Dick.
bios.Poe: American writer who helped invent detective fiction and modern horror.
bios.Angelou: American poet and memoirist known for I Know Why the Caged Bird Sings.
bios.Morrison: American novelist and Nobel laureate, author of Beloved.
bios.Marquez: Colombian novelist and Nobel laureate who wrote One Hundred Years of Solitude.
bios.Neruda: Chilean poet and Nobel laureate famed for his passionate love poetry.
bios.Rumi: Persian poet and Sufi mystic whose verses on divine love are read worldwide.
bios.Basho: Japanese poet who elevated haiku into a serious literary art form.
bios.Murasaki: Heian-era author of The Tale of Genji, often called the world's first novel.
bios.Ovid: Roman poet whose Metamorphoses retold myth after myth of transformation.
bios.Virgil: Roman poet whose epic The Aeneid told of Rome's mythic founding.
bios.Sappho: Ancient Greek lyric poet of Lesbos, celebrated for verses on love and longing.
bios.Achebe: Nigerian novelist whose Things Fall Apart reshaped African literature's place in the world.
bios.Tagore: Bengali poet and polymath, first non-European to win the Nobel Prize in Literature.

bios.Tesla: Serbian-American inventor whose alternating-current systems power the modern electrical grid.
bios.Edison: American inventor holding over a thousand patents, including the practical light bulb.
bios.Brunel: Victorian engineer of the Great Western Railway and daring bridges and steamships.
bios.Eiffel: French structural engineer who built the iron tower that became the symbol of Paris.
bios.Watt: Scottish engineer whose improved steam engine powered the Industrial Revolution.
bios.Stephenson: English engineer whose Rocket locomotive helped launch the railway age.
bios.Roebling: German-American engineer who designed the Brooklyn Bridge, dying before its completion.
bios.Telford: Scottish civil engineer renowned for roads, canals, and bridges across Britain.
bios.Ford: American industrialist who pioneered the moving assembly line for mass car production.
bios.Diesel: German engineer who invented the compression-ignition engine bearing his name.
bios.Otto: German engineer who built the first practical four-stroke internal combustion engine.
bios.Benz: German engineer credited with building the first practical automobile.
bios.Daimler: German engineer and inventor who pioneered the high-speed petrol engine.
bios.Whitney: American inventor whose cotton gin sped cotton processing but inadvertently entrenched slavery in the South.
bios.Arkwright: English inventor whose water frame helped launch the factory system.
bios.Nasmyth: Scottish engineer who invented the steam hammer for forging heavy machinery.
bios.Bessemer: English engineer whose steelmaking process made cheap mass steel production possible.
bios.Sikorsky: Russian-American aviation pioneer who built the first successful mass-produced helicopter.
bios.Whittle: British engineer and RAF officer who invented the turbojet engine.
bios.Carver: American scientist and inventor who revolutionized crop rotation and found uses for the peanut.
bios.GrahamBell: Scottish-American inventor who patented the first practical telephone in 1876.
bios.Marconi: Italian inventor who pioneered long-distance radio transmission.
bios.Wright: American siblings who achieved the first powered, controlled airplane flight.
bios.Fulton: American engineer who developed the first commercially successful steamboat.
bios.Gutenberg: German inventor whose movable-type printing press revolutionized the spread of knowledge.
bios.Archimedes: Ancient Greek engineer and mathematician behind the screw pump and principles of the lever.
bios.Heron: Ancient Greek engineer of Alexandria who built an early steam-powered device, the aeolipile.
bios.Lamarr: Austrian-American actress and inventor who co-devised frequency-hopping tech behind Wi-Fi and GPS.

default.blurb: Neutral fallback for unclassified or general-purpose work.

bios.Orion: The Hunter constellation, marked by a famous three-star belt visible worldwide.
bios.Vega: Brightest star in Lyra; was the north pole star around 12000 BCE and will be again.
bios.Rigel: Blue supergiant marking Orion's foot, one of the brightest stars in the night sky.
bios.Altair: Brightest star in Aquila and one point of the Summer Triangle asterism.
bios.Sirius: The Dog Star in Canis Major, the brightest star in Earth's night sky.
bios.Polaris: The North Star in Ursa Minor, sitting almost exactly above Earth's axis.
bios.Antares: Red supergiant heart of Scorpius, named rival of Mars for its ruddy glow.
bios.Cygnus: The Swan constellation, also known as the Northern Cross for its shape.
bios.Aquila: The Eagle constellation, home to the bright star Altair.
bios.Andromeda: Constellation of the chained princess, home to our nearest large neighbor galaxy.
bios.Cassiopeia: The Queen constellation, easily spotted by its zigzag W shape.
bios.Pyxis: The Mariner's Compass, a small and faint southern constellation.
bios.Carina: The Keel, part of the old giant ship Argo Navis, home to the bright star Canopus.
bios.Vela: The Sails, another piece of the dismembered ancient constellation Argo Navis.
bios.Corvus: The Crow, a small but distinct quadrilateral of stars.
bios.Auriga: The Charioteer, home to the bright golden star Capella.
bios.Bootes: The Herdsman, home to the bright orange star Arcturus.
bios.Hydra: The Water Snake, the largest of all 88 constellations.
bios.Phoenix: Constellation named for the mythical firebird that rises from its own ashes.
bios.Cepheus: The King, a house-shaped constellation near the north celestial pole.
bios.Lynx: A faint northern constellation named for a wildcat, said to be visible only to sharp eyes.
bios.Pavo: The Peacock, a colorful southern constellation.
bios.Tucana: The Toucan, home to the Small Magellanic Cloud galaxy.
bios.Grus: The Crane, a graceful southern-sky constellation.
bios.Ara: The Altar, a small southern constellation near Scorpius.
bios.Norma: The Level, a faint constellation named for a carpenter's square.
bios.Fornax: The Furnace, home to a rich and well-studied cluster of galaxies.
bios.Mensa: Table Mountain, the faintest of all 88 constellations, named after the peak in South Africa.
bios.Volans: The Flying Fish, a small southern constellation.
bios.Dorado: The Swordfish, home to the Large Magellanic Cloud galaxy.
bios.Sculptor: A faint southern constellation representing an artist's workshop.
bios.Caelum: The Chisel, one of the smallest and dimmest constellations.
bios.Pictor: The Painter's Easel, a faint southern constellation.
bios.Antlia: The Air Pump, a faint constellation named for the scientific instrument.
bios.Crux: The Southern Cross, the smallest constellation but a key navigation aid below the equator.
